data.title: S&P 500: Indicators Signal Rising Recession Risk
data.description:
We have been in recession watch for some time, but so far, the economy has proven resilient. Admittedly, part of the reason has been significant stimulus in the form of government deficits and a lowering of interest rates by the Fed. A few months ago it appeared that a "soft landing" would be achieved by the Fed and a recession averted, but more recently that scenario looks less likely, while a recession looks increasingly probable.

The market (SP500) appears to be starting to price the increased likelihood, in particular the discretionary sector (XLY). The discretionary sector tends to be the canary in the coal mine, the trend started with less affluent consumers impacting quick service restaurants including McDonald's Corporation (MCD) and even Starbucks Corporation (SBUX). More recently discretionary segments more linked to wealthier consumers such as travel have been giving downbeat guidance as well. Perhaps even more confounding and alarming, DoorDash, Inc. (DASH) is planning to let users buy now, pay later for fast food.

Recession vibes are also showing up in consumer sentiment surveys, with the University of Michigan sentiment index recently declining to 57 in March from 64.7 a month earlier, a more than two-year low.

At the same time long-term inflation expectations increased to a 32-year high. Inflation cycles are difficult to break, which is why it is surprising that Fed Chair Jerome Powell used it again to describe the potential impact of tariffs. It appears that the Fed will have to choose between a recession, or letting inflation get out of control. This will put the Fed between a rock and a hard place, as tariffs and elevated fiscal deficits put upward pressure on inflation at the same time that the economy and consumers are showing clear signs of weakness.

GDP Now

It is not only economists and public companies sounding the recession alarm, the Fed's real-time GDP forecast model is currently predicting a contraction of more than 3%. In just a few months it has gone from predicting values around 2.5% to a meaningful contraction.

Berkshire Hathaway Cash Hoarding

Warren Buffett, through Berkshire Hathaway Inc.'s (BRK.A)(BRK.B) wide collection of public and private companies, can gain great insights into the state of the economy. From rail transportation through BNSF, to several retail and manufacturing operations give Buffett a deep and unique perspective on the state of the economy.

Looking at how he has raised cash and short-term investments to more than $300 billion shows he is probably preparing for tougher economic conditions, which might give Berkshire the opportunity to deploy the capital at more attractive valuations. Notice that it is not just the absolute amount of cash and short-term investments that are high, but also when measured as a percentage of total assets.

Yield Curve

The 10Y3M yield curve has been inverted for some time now, and it has an excellent track record of forecasting recessions. The recession often arrives just after the inversion ends, which would mean a recession in the next six to twelve months could be expected.

Models that use the yield curve to estimate recession probabilities in the next twelve months, such as the Estrella-Mishkin model, have also been forecasting a recession.

Consumer Delinquencies

With consumer spending contributing more than two-thirds of the U.S. economic activity, clear signs of their financial weakening should be highly concerning for the overall health of the U.S. economy. Credit card and auto loan delinquencies are approaching levels last seen during the global financial crisis.

Business Bankruptcies

There has also been a recent spike in bankruptcies, and according to Bloomberg this week could top the 2009 record of 16 major business bankruptcies seen in a week. Some well-known companies that have recently filed for bankruptcy include 23andMe and Hooters. More generally, the small business uncertainty index is above the maximum level it reached during the worst of the Covid pandemic.

CapEx and Industrial Output

Weakness can be found beyond consumers, as the capital expenditures index is in contraction territory, with many businesses postponing investment decisions given the high uncertainty environment.

Unemployment

Putting additional pressure is the rising unemployment, which remains at a relatively low level in absolute terms, but has been trending higher since mid-2023. In fact, the Sahm Recession Indicator signal was triggered last year, as the three-month moving average of the national unemployment rate exceeded the previous twelve months' low by 0.5%.

Foreign Exchange

There are signs in the foreign exchange market that the risk of a U.S. recession is increasing, with haven currencies like the Swiss Franc (FXF) recently experiencing noticeable appreciation against the U.S. dollar.

Other Market Signals

There are a few other indicators that are similarly flashing recession warning signs. These include year-to-date marked outperformance of the consumer staples sector (XLP) in relation to the consumer discretionary sector, and widening spreads between corporate bond yields and Treasuries.

Takeaway

While accurately predicting a recession is extremely difficult, there are a number of reliable indicators that can help assess the probabilities. It is clear that in the last few months markets are pricing a significantly higher probability of a recession in the next twelve months. The "soft landing" scenario is looking increasingly complicated with the Fed expected to have less room to lower interest rates as inflation remains above target and is expected to further increase with tariffs.

Anecdotally, there is also increased evidence that the economy is rapidly weakening, with airlines, hotels, and restaurants giving disappointing guidance. Well-informed investors like Warren Buffett also appear to be increasing their cash reserves, with Berkshire Hathaway currently showing cash and short-term investments at the highest level relative to total assets in two decades. In any case, it is clear markets are increasingly pricing higher recession odds, with credit spreads widening, consumer staples outperforming the discretionary sector, unemployment trending higher, and increased loan delinquencies.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Trump's Liberation Day: What's Working In A Tariff Tantrum?
data.description:
The details of the tariff policy

After watching President Trump's "Liberation Day" speech on what tariff policy is going to look like going forward, some things stood out:

  • Reciprocal tariffs are the main point [the US will match foreign tariffs tit-for-tat] with some caveats.
  • 25% tariff on all auto imports.
  • The speech didn't seem like the tariffs were going to be removed any time soon unless a foreign country also removed their tariffs.
  • The final chart shown by President Trump seemed to indicate the reciprocal tariff basis may, in fact, be 50% of the average tariffs of other countries, which includes claims of currency manipulation that ratchet up the basis of other countries' tariffs against the US.

A lot was already known and baked into the recent downturn in the market. The interesting points straddled Trump referencing the 1920s US great depression being due to the US removal of tariffs and then trying to put them back on. This is a stance that seems like Trump does not want to remove this policy during his administration. While autos are the focus and major target, currency manipulation was also mentioned as an item that may force other countries to allow their currencies to gain strength against the dollar.

Below is what I'm happy to own, what I'm buying versus what I'm avoiding.

What's been working for me and what should continue to work

Oil & gas

Domestic US oil & gas plays have been a nice green shoot in the portfolios of many this year. My favorites are integrated oil majors and equipment names.

Among my top picks that I, personally, hold are as follows:

  • Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM).
  • Chevron Corporation (CVX).
  • Baker Hughes Company (BKR).
  • Halliburton Company (HAL).
  • Schlumberger Limited (SLB).
  • Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC).

Running an equal weight portfolio back test of these 6 up against the S&P 500 (SPY) total returns resulted in a 6.1% total return year to date vs -4.55% TR for the market. Here are the year-to-date total returns individually:

Of the bunch, I have 3 equipment names and one hybrid in Unit Corp that is a small-cap Oklahoma-based oil & gas company split between contract rig revenue and some domestic oil & gas interests. It currently has a 17+% dividend with no long-term debt. Domestic US equipment and oil names should see some benefits with tariffs on foreign energy. If domestic drilling is subsidized, I assume equipment names should be the biggest beneficiaries.

Waste Management

I started buying my 3 favorite waste management names last year in lump sums in anticipation of recession speak this year. To me, these are about as recession-proof a play as you can find, with no import or export issues, hence avoiding tariffs. They're not cheap, but they are some of the best compounders of the last decade and an excellent recession hedge. My Fab Trash 3 are:

  • Waste Management, Inc. (WM).
  • Republic Services, Inc. (RSG).
  • Waste Connections, Inc. (WCN).

Running an equal-weight portfolio back test of these 3 up against the S&P 500 (SP500, SPY) total returns resulted in a 16.1% total return year to date vs. -4.55% TR for the market. Here are the year-to-date total returns individually:

I last covered this portfolio idea here:

"How To Invest $10,000 In A 'Trash Stock' Portfolio Inspired By Oscar The Grouch."

I still believe it's one of the more attractive hedges out there. While most are pricey on a GAAP EPS multiple, they are much cheaper when looking at EV/EBITDA. They have a lot of non-cash expense items for truck depreciation and all have some capability of producing their own renewable energy through landfill methane re-capture. That gas is then used to power some of the fleet. If you live in the United States, check your waste management rates this year, they probably outpace inflation and the hikes for your other utilities.

REITs

REITs are still my favorite rate-sensitive play as a bond proxy. While there may be some currency return fluctuations for operators that have some overseas operations, for the most part, these are tariff-agnostic. I just stick to the big boys in my portfolio, all S&P 500 constituents with important rental real estate segments and avoid offices outside of Alexandria Real Estate Equities which I buy into the narrative that biotech office work will never be out of vogue. My favorite 5 are:

  • Realty Income Corporation (O).
  • Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc. (MAA).
  • VICI Properties Inc. (VICI).
  • Prologis, Inc. (PLD).
  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE).

Running an equal weight portfolio back test of these 6 up against the S&P 500 total returns resulted in an 8.91% total return year to date vs. -4.55% TR for the market. Here are the year-to-date total returns individually:

Foreign dividend ETFs

I've written a couple of articles regarding foreign dividend funds, namely the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) and Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYMI). The goal here is currency return. As dividends of blue chips ex-US are paid into the ADR [American Depository Receipt] trust or directly to the dividend stock ETF in foreign currency, those dividends will increase should the dollar depreciate when converted and paid to the investor.

As President Trump pointed out in the latter part of his speech today, currency manipulation is possibly incorporated into the assumed tariff basis for other countries. Should they not manipulate their currency and allow it to appreciate, that could result in tariff reductions under this assumption.

As we can see, this thesis has been working well so far and may have even more pent-up currency returns in the tank:

What I'm avoiding

Autos

Next to housing, these are the other high-ticket items that everyone needs to pay for. They are a target for tariffs here and abroad in retaliatory responses. Even though US autos are largely built in the US, their parts supply chain is a slew of imports that should also see higher costs unless they can move parts manufacturing to the US quickly. Not likely in my opinion. The consumer will probably have to eat the costs as most here already have thin margins and big CAPEX combined with a load of debt.

Toyota Motor Corporation (TM) and Honda Motor Co., Ltd. (HMC) are the best run in my opinion, with the best balance sheets of the legacy auto companies. They are also listed in Japan and in the US as ADRs, so they could see some currency return benefits should the dollar continue to depreciate. Most of their US models are made in the US but some are built in Canada.

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is in a league of its own when it comes to the balance sheet. However, big drops in foreign sales reported today, combined with a retaliatory tariff focus aimed right at anything CEO Elon Musk does, will not make this stock one for the faint of heart. I still believe Tesla will create some interesting new products in the next 3.5 years of Trump 2.0, but investors might want to digest another quarter or two of tariff talk before adding or buying the dip.

Hard asset Mag 7 stocks

Tesla, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) all qualify in my book as the Mag 7 stocks that sell the most tangible goods. These will also be subject to retaliatory tariffs more easily so than the asset-light companies. While I did buy into the Nvidia dip and a tad bit of Tesla [they have just been hammered, so I can't resist], I believe the Mag 7 ex those 3 are the safer options when looking to buy this anti-bubble.

Risks and summary

Trump is confusing the markets at present. Hopefully, this speech will be the definitive policy that we can all wrap our arms around. There are some great companies that are sure to be hurt by retaliatory tariffs. Travel decreasing could hurt energy and REITs, especially hotel and casino REITs like VICI. Foreign markets might take it on the chin a lot harder than we can visualize now, and foreign stock earnings plummeting could wipe out any currency return possibilities.

With a 25% auto tariff being the elephant in the room, I'd rather avoid the sector altogether for now. Physical US tech products will also be a target.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: United States Lime & Minerals: Still Not Ready For An Upgrade
data.description:
One company that I came across last year that, I thought, was really interesting was United States Lime & Minerals, Inc. (NASDAQ:USLM). As the company's name suggests, it operates in the lime and minerals industry. Specifically, it serves as a producer of lime and limestone products. These are ultimately sold to the construction, industrial, environmental, oil and gas, agricultural, and other industries. The company also sells its products to other industries such as those involved in the production of paper and glass, steel, roof shingles, and even poultry.

To many investors, a business such as this might not seem all that appealing. However, the more traditionally boring a company is, the more I find it to be attractive. In recent years, higher pricing has more than offset a reduction in output. This has allowed revenue, profits, and cash flows, to grow nicely. The firm also has no debt on its books and enjoys a net cash position of $278 million. For a firm with a market capitalization as of this writing of $2.60 billion, that's quite impressive.

Unfortunately, because of how the stock has been priced, I have not been able to rate it any higher than a "hold." Since my previous article about the firm in September 2024, the stock has pulled back by 3.4%. That compares to the 0.5% decline experienced by the S&P 500. Unfortunately, this downturn is not enough to justify an upgrade at this time. But for those with a long investment horizon, I do think it would be wise to continue to watch the business for a potential entry point.

No upgrade yet

2024 was a very interesting year for United States Lime & Minerals. Take revenue as an example. During that year, sales for the business came in at $317.7 million. That represents an increase of 12.9% compared to the $281.3 million the business reported the year before. You would think that such a sizable increase in a company that deals with a simple commodity would be the result of higher output. But that's not what happened. In fact, the company went from producing nearly 3.90 million tons of limestone to producing 3.85 million. This is nothing new for the firm. In 2022, limestone production was 4.02 million tons. This is part of a larger trend of lower production.

Management claimed that this reduction came from lower demand, mostly because of the construction space. This was offset to some extent by greater demand from industrial firms, environmental firms, and roof shingle customers. The decline in construction demand might seem peculiar. After all, total construction spending in the US in December of last year came in at an annualized rate of $2.20 trillion. That's up from the $2.12 trillion reported one year earlier. However, there is a difference between spending and overall activity.

When it comes to crushed stone, the total sold or used by producers ended up being 1.50 billion tons. That was down from the 1.55 billion tons reported for 2023. Total consumption actually dropped from 1.61 billion tons to 1.50 billion tons. The increase in revenue, then, came from higher pricing. Management does not actually offer pricing data per ton. But the USGS does. During 2024, the average price per metric ton was $17.50. That's a nice increase over the $15.86 reported the previous year. In fact, as the image below illustrates, the overall pricing for crushed stone, 70% of which is limestone, has risen quite a bit in recent years.

With higher revenue, we have seen higher pricing as well. Net income jumped from $74.5 million to $108.8 million. Operating cash flow also managed to climb, rising from $92.3 million to $126 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $101 million to $137 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company grew from $109.2 million to $149.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the final quarter of 2024 on its own relative to the same time in 2023. As was the case for the 2024 fiscal year in its entirety, the final quarter of the year showed remarkable year-over-year expansion in terms of revenue, profits, and cash flows. In fact, not a single cash flow metric for the company worsened from one year to the next.

If we use the data that management has provided, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. On a price-to-earnings basis, the stock does look very costly. The other two profitability metrics make it look more reasonable, though I would say that the firm looks to be near the high end of the fair value range using these. It is important to keep in mind, however, that this industry is incredibly resilient, offers attractive margins, and has had some nice growth in recent years. There's also the fact that the company has no debt and enjoys a net cash position of $278 million.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
United States Lime & Minerals, Inc. 23.9 19.0 15.6
Knife River Corporation (KNF) 26.6 16.6 12.6
James Hardie Industries plc (JHX) 23.9 12.7 12.3
Eagle Materials Inc. (EXP) 16.0 13.8 10.6
Martin Marietta Materials, Inc. (MLM) 14.9 20.4 10.2
Vulcan Materials Company (VMC) 34.6 22.4 18.2

I would say that all of this, combined, justifies some wiggle room when it comes to valuation. However, even when it comes to other similar firms, shares might be just a hair near the pricey end of the spectrum. In the table above, you can see how the firm stacks up against five similar companies. On a price-to-earnings basis, two of the firms were cheaper than United States Lime & Minerals, while another was tied with it. Three of the five companies I compared it to end up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. And when we use the EV to EBITDA approach, we can see that four of the five companies ended up being cheaper.

When it comes to the tariff situation that we are dealing with, there is both good news and bad news. The good news is that practically none of the crushed stone that the US produces from the roughly 3,500 quarries in its boundaries is exported. Only about 1% of what is consumed is imported. This means that there should not be any direct effects associated with tariffs on United States Lime & Minerals. Unfortunately, that's where the good news ends. The bad news is that tariffs will raise the prices of other things, including construction materials, vehicles, and more. Secondary and tertiary effects associated with tariffs will likely negatively affect demand in the construction space.

I actually think that there probably will be a reasonable delay in how this industry is impacted because construction projects are often planned months, sometimes years, in advance. But there is no denying that prolonged tariffs will reduce the demand in this industry. That could ultimately drive prices down, but we will have to wait and see the overall magnitude of this impact.

Takeaway

Currently, I think that United States Lime & Minerals is an intriguing company. However, it's not cheap enough to justify a bullish assessment as I see it. The stock looks pretty much fairly valued on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. Admittedly, it does have a great balance sheet, and growth in recent years has been encouraging. But until we see the stock price fall further or fundamentals improve even more, I believe that maintaining the company as a "hold" candidate makes the most sense.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: The Music's Still Playing -- But I'm Already Out
data.description:
Investment thesis

At first sight, it seems like SMCI has cleaned its mess, but it's still not out of the woods yet, because there are still open questions about management's practices and transparency. While I do not dismiss bullish signs and I recognize the low valuation of the company, my trust is fragile, and to get it back, I will need to see much more than a company that stayed listed on the Nasdaq and invited a Special Committee to conduct an investigation on its terms and at a time that was comfortable for them.

Reviewing Super Micro Computer’s past disclosures and corporate structure, I've found several areas that raise concerns for me, as for investor of this company, besides the accounting issue. For example, the structure of SMCI is complex, and executive roles are held by family members. Besides, the fact that Ernst & Young resigned, just adds to those concerns. From an investor perspective, it makes it harder to assess internal decision-making and long-term accountability. That uncertainty, for me, creates hesitation.

Even if I decide to forget about the past and look only forward, I am worried about the future investigation results that the SEC and DOJ will conduct, about the possibility that the numbers on its books aren't real and that one family holds a serious part of this business. All of these facts bring my non-systematic risk to unreasonable levels — and I did not even mention the razor-slim gross margins that continue to shrink.

As for me, I wouldn't short this stock, because I would like to leave space for the benefit of the doubt, but as an ex-investor in SMCI (I sold the stock at a higher level than it is right now — I sold for ~45 after the Hindenburg research came out), I am very happy that I sold. But if I hadn't, I would have sold now.

Company and situation overview

Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) is a US-based technology company that specializes in servers, storage systems, etc., for different needs like AI, cloud, and 5G. The company became one of the benefactors of the growing demand for AI infrastructure. SMCI used to build server systems for the cloud but got famous after its focus shifted to building servers that are entirely based on Nvidia’s GPUs. The growing Nvidia hype on the stock market brought huge popularity to SMCI, and as a result, there is probably no modern retail investor who has not heard of SMCI until today.

The problem is that most of those modern retail investors who bought SMCI stock after the company gained popularity as a result of the AI boom (me included!) did not know that the company had legal problems in the past. By the way, it was one of the very good lessons that I learned during my investing journey that started a little more than 4 years ago: when analyzing a potential investment, checking business and financials are not enough.

Back to the company, SMCI has a long history of legal and accounting issues. In 2018, the company was delisted from the Nasdaq because of the delay in filing the reports and was heavily investigated by the SEC. The bureaucracy of these processes is heavy, so it took long, but in 2020, the company was accused of violations in accounting practices from 2014 to 2017. The CFO of the company was accused of knowing about the issues, while not addressing them. The CEO did not get accused but was required to return to the company $2.1 million in stock profits:

According to the SEC’s order against Hideshima, he was on notice of these and other similar practices, yet failed to properly address them. The order also finds that Hideshima, who signed or approved filings with the Commission that contained materially misstated financial statements, knowingly circumvented certain of Super Micro's internal accounting controls. Super Micro's CEO, Charles Liang, while not charged with misconduct, is required to reimburse the company $2.1 million in stock profits that he received while the accounting errors were occurring, pursuant to the clawback provision of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act.

The story then ended with Howard Hideshima, the CFO at that time, stepping down without admitting or denying the findings. The company itself agreed to pay a fine of $17.5 million, without admitting or denying the SEC's findings:

Without admitting or denying the SEC's findings, Super Micro has agreed to cease and desist from violating Sections 17(a)(2) and (3) of the Securities Act of 1933 and the reporting, books and records and internal accounting controls provisions of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and pay a $17.5 million penalty. Hideshima, without admitting or denying the findings, has agreed to cease and desist from committing or causing violations of the reporting, books and records, and internal accounting controls provisions and pay disgorgement and prejudgment interest totaling more than $300,000 and a $50,000 penalty. Liang consented, without admitting or denying the findings, to reimburse Super Micro $2.1 million in stock sale profits.

It could be the end of the sad times for SMCI if not everything that we learned last summer.

To tell you honestly, now that I think about this, had I known before investing in SMCI that they’ve had legal problems in the past, I would anyway invest in this company, because well, problems happen. When I invested in SMCI metrics looked great - the valuation, growth, and profitability were ok. Nvidia was on board, so it screamed a perfect long-term investment. I would have probably believed that everything is on the former CFO, Howard Hideshima and that everything is in the past now. But when you trust someone and get disappointed for the second time, it doesn’t feel like a coincidence. So I asked myself, were the issues really connected to the former CFO or anyone else who was fired, and if those people left, why do the problems stay?

When I say problems, I don’t mean the delays in filing or even the accounting accusations. Let’s forget about it for a moment, because currently those issues are resolved. I refer to issues from the Hindenburg report that, for now, stay unaddressed, but the ongoing investigations are supposed to take them into account. Besides, the fact that Ernst & Young resigned, explained by the company being unreliable made me realize that the problem is indeed in the management that stayed after 2020 problems, and not the one that left:

EY’s decision to resign was rooted in recently uncovered information from this ongoing review, with the accounting firm stating that it could no longer rely on Super Micro’s management or Audit Committee representations. In a filing with the SEC, EY highlighted governance and transparency concerns, signaling a significant breakdown in trust with the AI server maker.

The DOJ and SEC both launched investigations into the company, major shareholders filed lawsuits, and all of these issues are not resolved yet as well. I do not expect it to be resolved in the next one and a half to two years at least, so when I read about investors and fellow analysts saying the company is out of the woods, I strongly disagree.

But as I already mentioned, I recognize not only the downsides. Despite the problems, SMCI does have real partnerships with major tech players. First, its hardware is used by AWS, Microsoft, and other hyperscalers. Second, the fact that SMCI continues to deliver solutions, which are using new technology like Nvidia’s Blackwell, states that the partnership is still ongoing, although I would like to hear some comments from Nvidia’s management on this matter. SMCI’s management wouldn’t be screaming about it if Nvidia had dropped them, but again, if the partnership would be done, they wouldn’t have free access to Blackwells by now. There is a possibility that Nvidia relies more heavily on SMCI’s competitors like Dell, but it’s still not confirmed.

Valuation

SMCI is undoubtedly valued at a discount. Its P/E ratio is almost 40% lower than the sector median, which suggests it's a decent buying opportunity. But it’s important to note that when we compare SMCI to peers like Dell or HPE, these numbers aren’t all that surprising. In fact, SMCI is trading at a premium relative to those peers (those companies are much larger, so it is logical). Of course, Dell and HPE are not only in AI, and not a high-density server architecture the way SMCI positions itself, but still, the valuation does not seem that positive in comparison. At least not to the extent of forgetting and forgiving past troubles.

The growth is very impressive, though! Revenue growth of 125% year-over-year compared to just 5% for the sector and far above any of the peers in the same sector. This is exactly the type of growth we were expecting back in the day when we were investing in AI companies, and SMCI is one of them. But… This metric is impressive if you fully trust the numbers management is reporting to their new auditor firm. Personally, I look at these numbers with skepticism. I might be wrong. But this is my article and my opinion.

Profitability is very weak, which suggests that the company is losing the pricing game. This is a huge disadvantage, and if HPE and Dell decide to take SMCI out, they can do so easily. SMCI’s gross margin is at 12%, while the sector median is about 50%, a huge difference. What’s even more concerning is the fact that this margin trend isn’t improving. In fact, it’s getting smaller and smaller. High growth with low and shrinking margins is a very unhealthy combination, especially in a competitive industry.

The Quant Rating is at 2.79, which is technically a HOLD, but it is getting close to being a SELL. And that makes sense when you look deeper: revision trends are mostly negative, profitability is deteriorating, and momentum isn't perfect. Even with the strong growth it seems like neither analysts are convinced, neither Quant nor me.

Macro & Sector

Besides the risks related to the company itself, let’s not forget about the risks coming from the macro conditions and the sector. First, it seems like the narrative about an unstoppable AI growth starts to shift. According to TD Cowen, Microsoft (MSFT) has recently backed out of over 2 gigawatts worth of planned data center capacity in the U.S. and Europe. TD Cowen analyst Michael Elias said that in their opinion, Microsoft decided not to do additional AI training with Open AI for now, and as a result, it will cut the number of data centers built. Moreover, they believe that Microsoft has even more than it needs for now. It doesn't mean that the demand will disappear, but it definitely raises questions about whether the growth of the AI industry will be as straight-lined as we've hoped. If it becomes a trend and hyperscalers one by one start cutting their capex, the sector will see a downturn of such a magnitude that it might seem irrational if we take into account the worry about AI being a bubble.

Another factor, of course, is economic conditions, but I won’t spend the time; most likely, you are following the recession/tariffs situation the same as I am.

Conclusion

There are so many companies on the market in every sector. You can easily find some undervalued options, valued fairly but growing, or overvalued but hyped. All of these choices I can understand depend on the specific situation. In the case of SMCI, I honestly don’t understand why investors would agree to a risk that is huge and unnecessary. You won’t get paid for the non-systematic risk you take. It means you choose to invest in a stock that has a very serious downside (delisting, problems with the authorities, fines, etc.) and at the same time has a very capped upside. The stock is volatile, so it definitely presents a nice trading opportunity if you are playing in and out, but in a game like this, you never know when you’ll be the one left without a chair. I wouldn’t hold this stock and wouldn’t short it. In my opinion, both are too risky. Claims about the company may be wrong, or they may be true, but I am not ready to stay on and experience this turbulence in my portfolio. Besides, Sara Liu, who is also the wife of the CEO and serves as SVP of SMCI, sold all of her shares in February 2025. While insider selling can happen for many reasons, it may raise additional questions for investors already concerned about the management and other issues, at least for me it does. As a result of everything described in this article, I am rating SMCI stock as a SELL and will write a follow-up article if the situation changes.

data.title: Four Myths About Tariffs, Plus Their Likely Impact
data.description:
This article talks about why the tariffs, enacted or imminent, will cause significantly more harm than good. It debunks a number of economic myths used to support tariffs. It details the impact of the enacted and announced tariffs. Finally, there is a section on how to play it as an investor.

The chart below shows estimates of the size of tariffs based on those currently in place or planned.

As shown above, until the 1900s tariffs were usually the primary source of revenue for U.S. government. Tariffs shot back up during the 1930s a period also known as the Great Depression. They have been at low levels since 1980, a period of strong economic expansion.

We’ll start with the myths about tariffs.

Myth 1: Reshoring

The U.S. unemployment rate is currently 4.1%, a historically low level. If it gets any lower it becomes inflationary. That’s why it almost never goes below 3.5% in the U.S. The last time we got to 3.5% was in 2022. No coincidence, inflation soared.

Let’s do some math. U.S. corporate revenue growth runs 5-10% a year. The growth of the U.S. workforce is only 1% a year, with almost all of that from immigration. Can you see the problem? U.S. corporations are growing much faster than the U.S. workforce. Sure, part of that gap is taken up by productivity gains. But there is still a big gap. Throw on top of that, Baby Boomer retirements exceeding young workers entering the workforce and a low birth rate. That’s why U.S. corporations have been sending jobs overseas for decades. It’s a demographics issue. The U.S. does not have the workforce growth to handle its incredible corporate growth. Frankly, it’s a good problem to have, one there is no need to fix.

Do you know how many jobs have been sent overseas? Neither do I. It’s not published. Forbes estimates 300,000 move overseas annually. TeamStage also says 300,000 a year annually. TeamStage further says China alone is responsible for 3.7 million of jobs outsourced from the U.S. Then there are tens of millions more created by U.S. corporations overseas that never were in the U.S. TeamStage says in 2015 alone, U.S. affiliates created 14.3 million new jobs overseas. Most of those were probably to serve the local markets, but some were to import back to or serve the U.S.

Let’s do some more math. The total number of jobs sent or created overseas is almost certainly tens of millions. At a 4.1% unemployment rate, the U.S. has about 6 million unemployed. But only maybe 1 million are available before reigniting labor inflation. The example of 2022 showed a 3.5% unemployment rate is inflationary. Again, only 1 million available workers for tens of millions of jobs created. The point is: The U.S. did not have the workforce to keep those jobs here. They had to be sent overseas! The only other solutions would be more immigration, more productivity, or slower corporate growth.

Of course, a recession changes things temporarily. The unemployment rate usually soars.

This leads to another myth - governments need to create jobs. Governments at the Federal, State, and local levels spend billions luring companies with incentives to create jobs. However, the U.S. no longer has a jobs problem except in certain high unemployment areas. The U.S. did need government help with jobs 50 years ago and even 20 years ago, but not now. What has changed? Demographics and massive corporate growth due to American Exceptionalism (discussed more under Myth 4). The demographics include a low birth rate, high baby boomer retirements, and a lower labor force participation rate.

Myth 2: The Trade Deficit

Another reason given for tariffs is to reduce the U.S. trade deficit. The U.S. trade deficit was $918 billion in 2024. Sounds like a lot, right? The following is a chart showing the trend for U.S. wealth by the Federal Reserve.

Do the math again. U.S. wealth has been increasing by about $10 trillion a year on average. The $1 trillion going out each year is 10% of that, little impact. Trade deficits are not hurting. It just expands foreign markets U.S. companies can sell to.

Myth 3: Foreigners Pay

Tariffs add to the cost of goods entering the country. The companies importing pay them. They then have to decide whether to eat that cost and be less profitable or pass it along to their customer. It will depend on the industry, but both are likely to happen to some degree. If the companies pass along the costs that usually reduces demand as fewer consumers are willing to pay the higher prices. Either way, bad for companies that import.

Myth 4: America Is Broken And Not Winning

It seems there is a secret about the U.S. that few Americans know about. It’s called American Exceptionalism. The U.S. has 4.3% of the world’s population. Can you guess what its publicly traded companies are worth as a percentage of the world? It’s not 5% or 10% or 20% or even 40%. Depending on how and when you measure it, it’s 50-65%. The U.S. is batting 12x above its population weight. Statistically, that is off the charts! What’s more, this percentage has actually been increasing for the past decade. This is not new. The massive outperformance, as shown in the chart below, has mostly been the case for 100 years. Japan had a moment in the 1980s and quickly fell back. China is still a blip. Europe? Well…

It gets better. The majority of all major inventions in the past 150 years have come from the U.S. This is still overwhelmingly the case. Take a look at the major innovations of the past 30 years.

  • AI
  • Drones
  • Smartphones
  • Cancer treatments
  • GPS
  • Social media
  • Obesity drugs
  • Electric vehicles
  • Gene editing
  • 3D Printing.

Almost all of these were primarily developed in the U.S. The only ones that weren’t are gene editing (a U.S. and French scientist won a Nobel Prize for that) and obesity drugs (half American, half Danish).

Tariffs As A Negotiating Tool

Another reason given for tariffs is as a negotiating tool. Tariffs as a negotiating tool didn’t work much last time with Europe or China. China’s concessions were superficial and not met. There was a new agreement with Canada and Mexico that took 3 years to negotiate and had only moderate changes. Success this time will require negotiating individually with each country. Since the U.S. economy is larger, it can bully every other country. But, that is not the case if countries team up. If countries team up, it becomes much harder. That may be happening.

On March 30, 2025, China, Japan and South Korea announced they are discussing responding to U.S. tariffs together. This, if followed through, could be devastating. Japan and South Korea have been 2 of the U.S.’s most stalwart allies and very useful in helping contain the threats posed by China. Japan and South Korea teaming up with China would be a major victory for China, and a major loss for the U.S. If the Europeans join them, the U.S. will have little leverage left.

Tariffs Benefits

There are a few benefits to tariffs. They create extra revenue for the Federal government, which will make a dent in the budget deficit. Prior tariffs do appear to have reduced reliance on China for manufacturing.

Tariff Impacts

The tariffs implemented and planned will likely create major problems, which are listed below.

1. Inflation – The higher cost caused by tariffs is paid by consumers, who are both individuals and companies that import goods. Reshoring as detailed earlier is wage inflationary.

2. Lower Corporate Profits – Many importing companies will not be able to pass along the higher costs to their consumers and will have lower profit margins. GM and Ford are two clear examples. Those that raise prices face lower demand, as some customers will balk at the higher prices.

3. Less Free Trade - Many investors are aware that the last time the U.S. had high tariffs was in the 1930s, caused in part by the Smoot-Hawley Act of 1930. The 1930s were the time of the Great Depression. I am not predicting that, but throughout history, high tariffs have usually led to economic weakness. U.S. economic growth has surged since around 1990 due in large part to the fall of the Berlin Wall and the opening up of China and Eastern Europe. Both led to much more free trade as there were many more trading partners.

4. Foreign Reprisals – Tariffs by the U.S. have already led to reprisals, and that will likely increase as U.S. tariffs increase. That hurts the revenues and earnings of U.S. corporations doing business overseas.

5. Less U.S. corporate growth – According to visualcapitalist.com, 28% of sales from the S&P 500 companies are outside the U.S. For the IT industry, it's 59%. That industry is where much of U.S. growth has come from in recent years.

6. Recession – Tariffs alone may not be enough to cause a recession, though with major reprisals, it may be. When combined with massive federal spending cuts, it’s more than enough. Let's do the math. The DOGE wants to cut at least $1 trillion in Federal spending. U.S. GDP was $27.7 trillion in 2023. A $1 trillion cut in spending is 4% of GDP. Our GDP growth has averaged 2-3% in recent years. While it's unlikely all those cuts would occur this year, a cut of that magnitude would still have a huge impact. The most vulnerable year is fiscal 2026, starting in October 2025. Also, keep in mind the magnifier effect. Every federal cut indirectly leads to job losses and spending cuts by the private sector.

As I wrote in my last article, Chaos May Lead To A Recession I believe the current policies of large tariffs combined with large spending cuts, and lower consumer sentiment are enough if continued to create a recession. In the last article, I felt there was time to avoid one. We are close to the tipping point now, as shown in the chart below.

It’s not as bad as it looks, but the trend is horrible. Most of the decline shown above is due to a surge of imports to front-run tariffs and also import gold. Even if you adjust for the gold imports (the broken line above), the last quarter now looks negative. Keep in mind, that last quarter was before much of the planned tariffs went into effect.

7. Loss of Allies – The tariffs have been primarily implemented on countries that were historically the U.S.’s closest allies. These include Europe, Canada, Japan and South Korea. All of them have shown signs of moving away from the U.S. The U.S. is moving toward the isolationist 1930s, the time of the Great Depression.

Reciprocal Tariffs

Most countries have certain industries they want to protect. The U.S. is no different, providing all kinds of subsidies to farmers, medical research, IT manufacturing, etc. Many countries do this with tariffs. There are 2 reasons reciprocal tariffs don’t work well. One is the 7 reasons listed above. It causes way more problems than it solves. In fact, it is not needed, the U.S. economy, as shown earlier in this article, is the envy of the world and currently has low unemployment. The second reason is that tariffs protect industries that are very sensitive to other nations. It is often politically and economically difficult for them to walk away from those protections.

Impact So Far

The chart below shows that the growth S&P 500 average earnings estimates for the current quarter have been dropping rapidly after surging last year. Second quarter 2025 EPS growth is now estimated at up 8% from over 14% mid-last year.

This indicates the economy is still not close to a recession but moving rapidly in that direction. This is a bottoms-up calculation that does not take into account the most recent tariffs and reprisals. Consumer and business sentiment are already at recessionary levels.

How To Play It

Domestic companies that may benefit are metals companies and manufacturers that source from and sell to primarily U.S.-based entities. Others not affected much by tariffs are utilities, REITs, consumer staples, and insurance (but watch their bond portfolios). Underweight any economically sensitive company. These include materials, subprime lenders and consumer discretionary. A lower weighting of IT makes sense. Avoid heavily leveraged and other riskier companies.

The problem with buying tariff beneficiaries such as metals companies is that any benefit they get from less competition may be offset by less customer demand. The stock prices of Nucor (NUE) and Alcoa (AA) are actually both down this year. So, there really are few clear beneficiaries. The best I can find is The Goodyear Tire & Rubber (GT). Goodyear manufactures most of what it sells in the U.S. and has a product less impacted by a recession than a steelmaker. There really are few places for an investor to hide.

Not having clear stock purchase choices, I have positioned my portfolio more conservatively by increasing cash and buying the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) along with a few small hedges. The TLT would benefit in a recession, as the Fed would be forced to lower interest rates even if inflation remains high. Recessions usually tame inflation anyway. I do a lot of short-term event-driven trades that should still be worth doing. For buy and hold investors, consider less leveraged companies paying higher dividends. Safe higher dividend stocks should benefit from the lower interest rates recessions usually bring.

Another reason to be conservative is, the market is still overvalued versus historical indices such as the P/E ratio.

The only potential good news I see is the administration backing off. Otherwise, there is a good chance the recession starts, and maybe as soon as this month.

Takeaway

U.S. corporations have been a massive growth engine for a long time, and growth has only accelerated in the past decade. A big part of this was driven by outsourcing overseas due to a slower growing workforce in the U.S. Tariffs would only slow things down by reducing access to labor and foreign markets, losing allies, reducing corporate profits, risking a recession, and increasing inflation. Investors’ best course is a more conservative risk-off approach to investing until there is more clarity.

data.title: CAVA Group: A Story As Pristine As Its Balance Sheet
data.description:
Echoes Of Peter Lynch

Peter Lynch is the famed Fidelity fund manager who generated 29% annualized returns during the 13 years he was at the helm of the Magellan fund.

Towards the end of his incredible run, he published a book entitled, "One Up On Wall Street."

I have read many investing books over the years; however, I treat One Up On Wall Street as something of a biblical text, in that I read it at least once per year, and I often read it more than once, returning to certain chapters every now and then, the act of which invariably inspires my investing in one way or another.

One of the key ideas he espoused throughout the book was to use "field research" to inform investment decisions, at least partially. He advocated for retail investors to use their firsthand knowledge of and experience with products or industries to find "the next big thing," or at least a good investment that performs well over time.

For my part, I've used this wisdom as often as I've been able to, and I've specifically used it in the restaurant stock space.

Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) has been one of my best investments, and my decision to investigate Chipotle as an investment was entirely driven by Mr. Lynch's wisdom and my affinity for Chipotle's food.

As CAVA Group, Inc. (NYSE:CAVA) prepared for an IPO in 2023, people close to me shared how much they loved the food that CAVA served; while there were no CAVAs in my city at the time, I certainly took note of their love for CAVA.

As the company IPO'd, I began reviewing its S-1. What I found was that its economics were virtually identical to those of Chipotle, a business on which I'd become an expert through my ownership of its stock and monitoring of its financials.

So I decided to drive an hour to the nearest CAVA to investigate it myself, and I found that it was, indeed, in virtually every respect, "The Chipotle Of Mediterranean Cuisine."

With a deep understanding of its finances and an appreciation for its food in mind, I decided to include the stock in the LAS Index when it was launched, at about $30/share, and it has performed brilliantly since.

I share all of this to say:

  1. Peter Lynch's One Up On Wall Street works! I highly recommend reading the book, then re-reading it; perhaps every year like me, granted you enjoy the sport of investing.
  2. Winning stocks are likely all around you: it just takes a little analysis of the products and businesses with which you interact on a routine basis.
  3. Chipotle and CAVA are dominant brands in the 21st century, whose economics are virtually identical and whose formats are as close to identical as is humanly possible. Both have about 25% restaurant-level margins. Both have huge, industry-leading (way above the competition) same-store sales growth. Both have healthy average unit volumes at about $3M a store. They're essentially the same business serving the same thing, except one is Mexican and the other Mediterranean… but even there, we see Chipotle serves Mexican, and CAVA serves the Mediterranean. The similarities are striking indeed.

CAVA's Brilliant 2024 (Note The 25% Restaurant Level Margin, Which Is Identical To Chipotle's Unit Economics, As Well As The 13.4% SRS Metric, Which Is Much Higher Than Industry Averages)

Related to my third point, in the data below, I provided a comparison of CAVA to Chipotle using a couple of data points. I included revenue growth for Chipotle dating back to its IPO, as well as unit count growth dating back to its IPO, and did the same for CAVA.

Chipotle's All-Time Growth Rate & Margins Since Going Public

Chipotle Unit Count Since IPO

And, below I included CAVA's current growth rate, as well as its current unit count.

CAVA Revenue Growth Rate & Free Cash Flow Margin (Roughly Profitability)

CAVA Restaurant Count

CAVA has continued, since it IPO'd, to exceed even my loftiest projections for growth. Based on its most recent report, as well as guidance for 2025, it appears that it's only picking up steam.

Adjusting for an idiosyncratic accounting event, CAVA actually grew at 36% in Q4 '24, while its margins expanded such that it generated free cash flow for Q4 and for the full year 2024, supported by its incredibly strong, Chipotle-like unit economics.

This model is super powerful. Our top quartile of restaurants deliver restaurant-level margins above 30%, but we want to make sure that we're making the right investments, leaning into the right things at this stage of our growth, so that we're positioning the business for sustainable long-term success.

Tricia Tolivar, CFO, CAVA Q4 '24 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

Incredible.

I mean this is software-like growth and margin expansion for a relatively boring restaurant concept (if you're consistently reading my work, you'll know that I also find most software stocks to be relatively boring. It's just infrastructure sold to businesses, not something so fantastical that it should scare sober investors away).

And this torrid pace of growth is set to continue in 2025.

Now to our outlook for the full year 2025, we expect the following: 62 to 66 net new CAVA restaurant openings, CAVA same-restaurant sales growth of 6% to 8%, CAVA restaurant-level profit margin between 24.8% to 25.2%, preopening costs between $14 million and $15 million and adjusted EBITDA, including the burden of preopening costs, between $150 million to $157 million.

Tricia Tolivar, CFO, CAVA Q4'24 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

As context for the openings this year, below, we can again see CAVA's progress in unit count:

CAVA Restaurant Count

So, by the end of 2025, CAVA will likely have fielded well over 400 total locations.

In its IPO filings (S-1), it projected 1,000 locations by about 2030, and it's more than on track to meet that goal.

To this end, what's most interesting to me about CAVA is that the brand feels very international.

That is, if KFC and Taco Bell can do well in international markets, selling rather pedestrian fare, then CAVA should excel in markets like Australia, Europe, Asia, and Latin America though only time will tell.

However, it's important to consider this today, as the 1K location count target was only for North America; not the rest of the world.

The value of a business today is the present value of all the cash flows it will generate over time, so to understand those future cash flows, and their potential size, we must understand, or envision to some degree, what a company's runway for growth is, and, in the case of CAVA, I believe it is very long, especially when we consider international expansion opportunities over time.

Speaking of cash flows, let's turn to a brief valuation exercise, and then we will wrap up with some risk considerations.

Valuation

My assumptions are as follows:

Assumptions:

TTM Revenue [A]

 $1B

Potential Free Cash Flow Margin [B]

15%

Total diluted shares outstanding [C]

121M

Free cash flow per share [ D = (A * B) / C ]

$1.24

Free cash flow per share growth rate

22.5%

Terminal growth rate

3%

Years of elevated growth

10

Total years to stimulate

100

Discount Rate (Our "Next Best Alternative")

9.8%

And, below, we can see my model:

Discounted Cash Flow Model + Assumptions Input + Dilution Considerations

As we can see, I used a 22.5% annualized growth rate, which is aggressive to be very sure.

That said, CAVA has been accelerating growth, raising guidance, and beating all guidance set forth previously.

In order to achieve this growth rate, CAVA would need to be one of this century's defining fast food brands: what McDonald's was to the 20th century and what Chipotle has been in the first quarter of this century.

And I frankly believe it is that.

There's nothing else out there like it in terms of what it offers and, importantly, in terms of its execution prowess, which has been proven to be brilliant.

It's no small feat to scale a restaurant business to 400 locations in a decade; in fact, there are likely millions of great restaurants that have the cuisine and concept necessary for such scale, but they simply lack the will or competence to scale to that level.

Again, no small feat.

I also reduced CAVA's share count over the next ten years, implying that CAVA will begin a share buyback in the next 5 or so years, and I'm confident it will:

It has $366M in cash on its balance sheet and no debt. It generated free cash flow sustainably in 2024.

It will soon have ample cash which it won't need lying around. It doesn't need to empire build by buying adjacent businesses: its runway for growth is giant, especially when we think about international opportunities. Buying adjacent concepts would only serve to dilute focus and execution, harming shareholders in the process.

So I believe the 10% reduction in share count is prudent.

Lastly, I used a 15% free cash flow margin, which, to be sure, is well above CAVA's current free cash flow margin; however, because I know Chipotle so well, and because Chipotle's unit economics, identical as they are to CAVA's, have supported a 15% free cash flow margin, I believe this is acceptable to assume.

Turning to projected returns…

Total Projected Returns

I used a 35x exit multiple, which, I believe, is fair, though it might actually be conservative, considering the aforementioned runway for growth and CAVA's very high potential to sustain elevated growth well beyond 2033, similar to how Chipotle has grown at 15% far, far longer than I believe many anticipated it would.

And so 15% returns are attractive.

I would say, more than anything, this exercise just supports accumulating CAVA over time, as it dips during market panics, such as the one we're experiencing presently.

Summary: Risks And Buying Strategy

I'll say CAVA has been on a tear, though it is down from its lofty $150/share share price.

I'm certainly not jumping for joy at the opportunity we're being afforded here. I do like CAVA here, but I'm not over the moon about it due to its valuation. That is, this isn't a major market dislocation where market participants aren't seeing the value of CAVA: they get it. The market gets it, and the exceptional performance has been priced into the stock via an exceptional (high) valuation.

That said, my philosophy on CAVA has become:

As what will likely be considered a defining brand, akin to Chipotle or Netflix, I believe it makes sense to routinely accumulate the stock via a dollar-cost average strategy.

I've often thought, "I should just set up a DCA and blindly buy this every quarter for the next five years, then let it ride from there."

All of this said, there's of course risk. CAVA needs exceptional, pristine execution from management to grow at an aggressive 20%+ rate for 10 years straight. To expand internationally will be no small feat as well.

And, so, while I like the strategy I just shared above for those wanting to own CAVA, it's worth remembering that I often discuss growth stocks and growth stocks are risky, and the principal way to mitigate that risk is through healthy diversification.

In brief, I like CAVA and believe it will be higher in 5-10 years than where it currently trades.

data.title: Buy AMD Stock Now To Capitalize On The Generational AI Megatrend
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock, it has lost 6.4% in price, opening up a further buying opportunity.

AMD is one of the integral companies positioned at the nexus of a generational shift in economic history driven by AI. Global AI infrastructure spending is exploding, and the market is becoming more diverse. I envisage a Fiscal 2027 fair price for AMD stock of about $215, but I'm setting a take-profit of $200 because I think a severe overvaluation could occur well before this. Risk-wise, I find it incredibly important at the moment to manage a black swan portfolio. The Taiwan crisis risk is the primary factor that makes any portfolio a house of cards at present. Until the newly planned TSMC fabs are fully online in the USA, which may take years, equity investing is a high-risk pursuit which requires advanced hedging strategies.

Macro & AMD-Specific AI Growth Analysis

With hyperscalers investing unprecedented capital into AI infrastructure, a multi-decade growth trajectory for data center compute has become apparent to me. In aggregate, across Big Tech data center projects, nearly $400 billion will likely be spent on AI infrastructure globally in 2025. This is not a one-time spike. AMD's CEO Lisa Su has already noted that in data centers alone, the AI accelerator TAM is projected to grow at over a 60% CAGR to $500 billion in 2028. This is roughly the size of the entire semiconductor industry in 2023.

The bear case argues that AI investment might plateau after the initial rush. If companies find current AI models "good enough" or achieve efficiency gains, they might slow spending. DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) is one such company that has caused concerns from the market about smaller startups training competitive AI models cheaply, raising questions about the necessity of high AI capex. The consensus among Big Tech leaders appears to be to the contrary: increased efficiency will increase AI adoption. Essentially, the TAM will expand. This will include new major players like nation states. With AI becoming a utility, we're essentially seeing the foundation laid for what could be an indefinite build-out with long-term iterations into robotics and advanced autonomy.

AMD is well-positioned to capitalize on this trend, which should be the foundation for why anybody buys the stock at its current cheap valuation. Over the past 10 years, AMD has morphed itself into a provider of high-performance central and graphics processing units, as well as adaptive accelerators. AMD is one of the only companies capable of designing market-leading AI accelerators.

In 2023, AMD cemented its position as an integral AI infrastructure provider by launching its flagship Instinct MI300 series graphics processing units and accelerated processing units specifically aimed at AI training and inference in data centers. AMD's ROCm software stack, which acts as an open-source alternative to Nvidia's (NVDA) CUDA, is also foundational to its acting as integral linchpin of the AI revolution. ROCm is an example of the company lowering the barrier to adoption for AI, further expanding its TAM and increasing the penetration of AI technology across the globe with ease for consumers.

Following on from these strategic operational initiatives, AMD's data segment surged by 94% year-over-year in 2024 to $12.6 billion, primarily due to the ramp of Instinct MI300 AI accelerators and EPYC server CPUs. By Q4 2024, data center sales were up 69% year-over-year to a record $3.9 billion for the quarter; Instinct GPU shipments were a key driver.

Alex Karp, the CEO of Palantir (PLTR), one of the most powerful AI companies of the modern age, recently stated that the coming age "will be divided between AI haves and have-nots." It's worth encouraging the potential future have-nots to adopt AI now, because it appears essentially certain to me that this autonomous revolution will permeate and transform the global economy over the long term. Inequality of AI will likely be a real challenge of socioeconomics, but despite this concern, companies like AMD will very likely be at the epicenter of insatiable demand for status driven by AI efficiency and the associated monetary accrual. As a macro thesis, there is no other better industry to invest in right now; as a single-stock thesis, AMD is also attractively-valued and opportunistically-positioned.

Catalyst Analysis

Future growth catalysts for AMD include the full-scale ramp of MI300 series accelerators in 2025, shipping to multiple cloud providers and potentially enterprise customers and original equipment manufacturers. Later in 2025, AMD may introduce a successor; for example, MI350 series on 5nm/4nm with HBM3e. EPYC CPUs, such as the fifth generation "Turin," are also going to penetrate cloud and enterprise markets with increased performance and efficiency. Moreover, client and gaming innovations like the release of Ryzen processors with integrated AI capabilities and a refresh in the Radeon GPU lineup may stimulate demand for the segment.

Nations like India, Japan, France, and the United Arab Emirates are subsidizing domestic computing infrastructure and AI model development now, so we can expect AMD to be a key beneficiary of this trend. AMD PRO Technologies integrate enterprise-grade security and manageability features into processors, so this will be highly sought-after by sovereign (nation-state) AI developments. India aims to position its country as a global AI leader by 2030. Japan unveiled a $65 billion plan to support its domestic semiconductor and AI industries in 2024. France has announced a €109 billion investment plan in AI to bolster Europe's presence in the industry. AI is expected to contribute $96 billion to the UAE economy by 2030, accounting for 14% of its GDP.

Valuation & Price Targets

In my opinion, this is about the best time that one could invest in AMD stock. The price is below the 200-week moving average with an RSI value of ~35. That indicates extremely good value, and if you look back to July 2022, you will see the price hovered around the same dynamics before a strong bull run over the next 24 months. Given that AMD's near-term financial future is hinged on continued growth, the fundamental outlook is not one of decline, so the recent contraction is really sentiment-based and due for a substantial rebound over the next 24 months. Macro conditions, including a substantially lower interest rate environment likely to have occurred by and in FY26, will be highly contributory to such a rebound.

I'm targeting Fiscal 2027 revenue of $45 billion with a 30% net margin, equaling a net income of $13.5 billion. With diluted shares outstanding of 1.9 billion, this means a $7.11 EPS and an assumed terminal P/E ratio of 30. My stock price target is, therefore, $215. AMD is one of my largest positions, I have set a $200 take-profit as I think a significant overvaluation could occur ahead of time, potentially in H1 2026, and I would like to take profits ahead of fair value if this occurs. I will potentially re-buy the stock later if the valuation collapses again.

Risk Analysis

The long-term macro AI growth thesis is not without risks. The main of these pertains to a change in sentiment from AI hyperscalers and other customers around the utility of autonomy projects. ROI may have moments of weakness, but more importantly, a lack of clear vision and even moral hazards related to AI misuse at scale could cause serious clampdowns on the company's growth. I think AI operators have to be really clear on a well-defined set of values to penetrate through their AIs. The notion that AIs will be "value-independent" appears unbelievable to me. As such, the code that these systems are designed on needs to be fine-tuned with appropriate stewardship.

I consider the values at the foundation of the models to be the greatest threat to their long-term flourishing and acceptance by global markets. For example, if AIs running on AMD chips start performing autonomous actions which lead to the mass-scale firing of government or private-sector employees without full consideration of the socio-economic backdrop and consequences, AI could be largely deemed more dangerous than positive. The onus is on the AI programmers, fine-tuners, and architects at this fork in the road. Spirituality and value development seem like they will become more important than ever in the AI age.

Unfortunately, AMD's revenues will be directly caught in the crosshairs if AI misuse leads to severe regulatory crackdown and shutdown of AI. The threat of AI misuse will grow as AI agents proliferate, concentrating power in the wealthy who can afford the most powerful AI agents, making their values deeply penetrate into society for better or worse.

We should also starkly remember the major black-swan-style crisis which could occur in the next few years. I'm heavily invested in AI right now in my Nasdaq high-alpha black swan portfolio, but this is a house of cards. I'm 30% in cash, but an invasion or blockade of Taiwan by China's People's Liberation Army would lead to a drawdown of 50-90% of my portfolio value. I'd deploy my 30% cash position at the bottom into stocks I analyze as prudent at the time, largely negating the negative implications of the crisis on my portfolio. However, given that one simple blow can have such catastrophic effects on portfolios at this time, and the TSMC (TSM) plants outlined recently by the Trump administration are going to take years to be operational, I'm considering increasing my cash and bonds position to 40% for the time being.

These are perilous times amid a massive AI growth opportunity. Let's not forget the macro catalysts which could send our returns into a tailspin, and always prepare for the worst.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

AMD is a central player in what looks like a once-in-a-generation economic and technological shift toward AI. AI is not a fad—it's a fundamental driver of tech investments for the foreseeable future. Compute demand for AI is skyrocketing, and AMD has positioned itself as a primary provider. My Fiscal 2027 price target of $215 is conservative, and given the market's emotional nature, I'm betting a price of $200 will occur within the next 18 months. As a result, I've set a $200 take-profit point in my own holdings to capitalize on a potential overvaluation and trade in and out of the long-term thesis.

Remember though, these are perilous times geopolitically, and until America's manufacturing base is more secure, I'm holding 30% or more in cash because an invasion of Taiwan would devastate portfolios, especially tech-oriented ones like mine. The irony is that if you're prepared for the Taiwan crisis, you can come out with more money than if it never happened in the first place. I'll let you do the math on 40% of a portfolio in cash and bonds, liquidated after a 90% drop and invested in equities, appreciating back to what the equity values were pre-crisis. Let's hope it doesn't happen, but current conditions dictate prudence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Intuit: Steady Performance, But This Tax Leader Already Commands A High Price (Upgrade)
data.description:
Tax season has come, and despite sharp market volatility, Intuit Inc. (NASDAQ:INTU) is faring relatively better than most. The parent company behind TurboTax and Credit Karma has benefited from its decision to diversify its revenue streams between its consumer and enterprise arms to reduce its dependency on the consumer tax season.

Year to date, shares of Intuit are roughly flat, while many of the stock’s peers in the software sector have entered into bear market territory, down 20% or more from peaks. The question we have to ask is: does Intuit deserve this outperformance, and can it continue?

I last wrote a sell opinion on Intuit in December, when the stock was still trading at similar ~$630 levels. Since then, Intuit has largely hung on to its value amid strong fiscal Q2 (January quarter) performance, which is a good lead-up for the fiscal third quarter that generates the lion’s share of Intuit’s revenue. With this in mind, I am upgrading Intuit to a neutral rating.

Intuit’s accelerating revenue, especially at a time when many software peers have seen a much sharper deterioration in growth trends, makes me more sanguine on the company’s prospects: but at the same time, I see a relatively balanced bull and bear case for this stock. On the positive side for Intuit:

  • Intuit has succeeded at building diversified revenue streams. Its recurring revenue businesses like QuickBooks and Credit Karma have diminished Intuit’s reliance on the volatile tax season, and given the company high-margin recurring revenue.
  • Expansion potential within the install base: Though Intuit is already a well-known and saturated provider across all of its core products, it has managed to bake in potential upsells into each of its product lines, such as the ability for partial tax assistance.

At the same time, however, we should be wary of the following risks:

  • Intuit is heavily saturated. Where can it go from here? The company’s core product, TurboTax, has nearly universal recognition. The people who are comfortable with software-assisted DIY filing have largely already chosen their provider, between TurboTax and H&R Block. In the long run, the company’s growth rates will be limited to its expansion in non-tax initiatives
  • Heavy competition for non-tax products: Meanwhile, the company’s goal to push further into non-tax business software faces stiff competition. In particular, Quickbooks is up against the likes of Workday, Inc. (WDAY), Oracle Corporation's (ORCL) NetSuite, and SAP SE (SAP), all of which are better known for their finance suites.

The biggest reason I’m sidelined on Intuit, however, is its overrated valuation multiple, especially in comparison to rather paltry earnings growth.

For the remainder of FY25, Intuit has guided to $19.16-$19.36 in pro forma EPS. Meanwhile, for FY26, Wall Street analysts are currently expecting Intuit to generate $22.22 in pro forma EPS, or 15% growth against the midpoint of Intuit’s FY25 EPS range, on 12% growth in revenue - already representing no deceleration from this year’s growth expectations.

This puts Intuit’s valuation multiples at:

  • 31.8x FY25 P/E.
  • 27.6x FY26 P/E.

It’s perplexing to me why Intuit merits a premium valuation, especially over other bellwether software stocks like Adobe Inc. (ADBE) and Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), when it has already covered a large swath of its available market and its growth has slowed to a decent, but not altogether impressive mid-teens rate.

We must be especially vigilant in today’s stock market, being very selective in stock picking. Intuit is neither a star growth stock nor a cheap value stock: and stuck in that no man’s land, it doesn’t belong in my portfolio just yet.

Q2 download

Let’s now go through Intuit’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The chart below showcases the company’s trended results by business segment:

Intuit’s revenue grew 17% y/y to $3.96 billion. The bright side for Intuit: the company’s results dramatically beat Wall Street’s much more pessimistic expectations of $3.77 billion (+11% y/y) by a major six-point margin. We note as well that Intuit’s revenue growth accelerated sharply over the 10% growth that it showed in Q1.

The company’s Global Business Solutions unit, which represents slightly over half of Intuit’s annual revenue, drove the majority of that acceleration, with growth revving up from 9% in Q1 to 19% in Q2 (though the company did note that a tough FY24 comp in Q1 depressed this segment’s growth rate last quarter). The company saw strength across all of its core business software solutions: QuickBooks Accounting, payroll and AP/AR services, and Mailchimp, the company's email marketing automation platform that it acquired in 2021 for $5.7 billion. We should be wary of possible deceleration in Mailchimp, however. The company is lapping price increases that it kicked off in Q2 of last year; on top of this, many businesses have slowed down their spending on growth marketing in the wake of a weaker macroeconomy, which has caused a drag on marketing software peers like Sprout Social, Inc. (SPT).

The consumer unit also showed nine points of sequential acceleration to 3% y/y growth, which is a healthy indicator ahead of the Q3 tax season. Management notes that its go-to-market approach, highlighting the new AI-assisted features, has led to a strong early start to the consumer tax season. Per CEO Sasan Goodarzi's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Let's shift to our durable go-to-market approach. We have reinvented our marketing campaigns focused on experience, speed to money, and price. We've strengthened our overall AI-driven personalized lineup and monetization capabilities. We are seeing great traction early in the season in DIY across simple and complex customers, with strong monetization driven by benefits such as early access to refunds and AI-powered human experts.

In the assisted category, with the significant improvement in experience, speed to money, and our "Beat Your Price" campaign, our early full-service funnel is strong. This is driven by marketing that started in the fall and improvements in local search for those looking for a pro near them. We estimate that our local experts in 130-plus designated market areas will give us access to approximately 80% of nationwide assisted filers, and we have found that filers are historically 5x more likely to convert when given a local option. We are also seeing more than 3x higher starts on the Credit Karma platform, driven by an increase in the availability of seamless zero-click login to TurboTax from 5% last season to 70% this season.

In summary, we are off to a strong start in tax. We are seeing strong growth across simple and more complex returns as the season progresses and strong overall average revenue per filer. We are also pleased with the power of one consumer platform given the seamless experience across TurboTax and Credit Karma."

Meanwhile, on the profitability front, Intuit has also successfully deployed AI across its service organization as well. It notes that customer contact rates for TurboTax product support have dropped 20%. Alongside using GenAI coding assistants in its product organization, the company notes that it has delivered $90 million in annualized run-rate operating expense savings by using AI. As a result, pro forma operating margins rose 230bps y/y to 31.8%. The company's pro forma EPS of $3.32 in Q2 also beat Wall Street's expectations of $2.56 with a whopping 30% upside.

Key takeaways

There's no doubt that Intuit is offering healthy and consistent performance, which can be a relief in a period of turmoil for many other enterprise software stocks that are more prone to macro-driven slowdowns. And yet, at the same time, we think Intuit's ~30x P/E multiple already more than compensates the company for its strengths.
